_note: Hand-curated badminton player generations. Edit and re-run scripts/seed-generations.ts to apply changes.

generations.label: The Sidek Brothers Era
generations.description: Malaysia's first golden generation. The Sidek brothers — Razif, Jalani, Misbun, Rashid — along with Foo Kok Keong and Cheah Soon Kit, dominated the Thomas Cup and put Malaysia on the world map in the 1980s and early 90s. Razif and Jalani won the 1992 Olympic bronze in doubles; Misbun was world No.1 in singles.

generations.label: The Lee Chong Wei Era
generations.description: Anchored by Lee Chong Wei — 3× Olympic silver medalist, world No.1 for a record 348 weeks. This generation also produced Kien Keat Koo and Tan Boon Heong (back-to-back All England doubles champions 2006–07), Goh Liu Ying (2016 Olympic mixed doubles silver), Wong Mew Choo, and Eei Hui Chin.

generations.label: The New Wave
generations.description: Malaysia's next golden generation. Lee Zii Jia won the 2021 All England. Aaron Chia and Soh Wooi Yik claimed Olympic bronze in Tokyo. Pearly Tan and M. Thinaah became the first Malaysian pair to reach a World Championship women's doubles final. This generation is expected to dominate through the 2028 Los Angeles Olympics.

generations.label: The Golden Era
generations.description: Indonesia's most decorated generation — Taufik Hidayat (2004 Olympic gold), Markis Kido & Hendra Setiawan (2008 Olympic gold), Nova Widianto & Lilyana Natsir (multiple world champions in mixed doubles). This era saw Indonesia dominate the Thomas Cup with some of the most flamboyant players the sport has ever seen.

generations.label: The Next Generation
generations.description: Kevin Sanjaya Sukamuljo and Marcus Fernaldi Gideon — the 'Minions' — redefined men's doubles with explosive net play, spending years ranked No.1. Anthony Sinisuka Ginting and Jonatan Christie lead the singles revival. Greysia Polii and Apriyani Rahayu won Olympic gold in Tokyo 2020.

generations.label: The Lin Dan Era
generations.description: The most dominant generation in badminton history. Lin Dan — 2× Olympic champion, 5× World Champion — is widely regarded as the greatest of all time. Chen Long won 2016 Olympic gold. Zhao Yunlei won 2 Olympic golds. China swept all five disciplines at the 2012 London Olympics.

generations.label: The New Guard
generations.description: Shi Yuqi and He Bingjiao lead China's current wave. Wang Yilyu and Huang Dongping won Tokyo Olympic gold in mixed doubles. Li Junhui and Liu Yuchen are perennial men's doubles challengers. Chen Yufei won 2020 Olympic gold in women's singles. Jia Yifan has been a dominant force in women's doubles for over a decade.

generations.label: The Lee Yong-dae Era
generations.description: Lee Yong-dae — 2008 Olympic gold in mixed doubles, 2012 Olympic silver in men's doubles — was the face of Korean badminton. Son Wan-ho was a consistent world-class singles player. Jung Jae-wook formed one of Korea's most successful doubles partnerships.

generations.label: The An Se-young Generation
generations.description: An Se-young — 2024 Paris Olympic gold medalist and world No.1 — headlines Korea's exciting current generation. Kim Won-ho and Seo Seung-jae are a formidable doubles partnership. Chung Eui-seok and Baek Ha-na have been competitive on the world circuit.

generations.label: Denmark's Golden Generation
generations.description: Viktor Axelsen — 2021 Olympic champion, 2× World Champion, ranked No.1 for record periods — is the most dominant men's singles player of his era. Anders Antonsen and Jan O. Jorgensen have also held world No.1. Denmark is the most successful non-Asian badminton nation in modern history.

generations.label: Japan's Rising Wave
generations.description: Japan's most successful badminton generation. Kento Momota dominated men's singles as world No.1 pre-pandemic with 2 World Championship titles. Akane Yamaguchi is a multiple World Champion. Yuki Fukushima and Sayaka Hirota have been one of the world's top women's doubles pairs for years.

generations.label: Thailand's Modern Era
generations.description: Kunlavut Vitidsarn — 2023 World Champion — is the face of Thailand's new generation. Ratchanok Intanon won the 2013 World Championship at just 18 years old. Dechapol Puavaranukroh and Sapsiree Taerattanachai dominated mixed doubles for a decade, spending years ranked No.1.

generations.label: India's Breakthrough Generation
generations.description: The generation that put India on the badminton world map. PV Sindhu — 2× Olympic medalist and 2019 World Champion — is one of India's most decorated athletes. Satwiksairaj Rankireddy and Chirag Shetty reached men's doubles world No.1 in 2023. Lakshya Sen won the 2022 All England at just 20.
